eli3: A RAG pipeline is the full system that connects your documents to an LLM so it can answer questions about them. The pipeline has three main stages: indexing (process and store your documents), retrieval (find relevant pieces given a question), and generation (use those pieces to write an answer).

Building each stage correctly is a significant engineering task. Indexing involves choosing how to split documents, which embedding model to use, and which vector database to store them in. Retrieval involves tuning how many results to fetch and how to rank them. Generation involves designing the prompt that combines the question with the retrieved text.

Each stage has multiple configuration choices that significantly impact quality. A well-designed RAG pipeline can answer questions accurately with citations; a poorly designed one will retrieve irrelevant chunks and produce hallucinated answers.

whenWhy.title: Enterprise document Q&A over private knowledge bases
whenWhy.body: The primary use case for production RAG: employees asking questions about internal documents (policies, contracts, technical docs, past projects) that the LLM was not trained on. The RAG pipeline enables: accurate answers grounded in the actual documents, source citations for verification, access to documents updated after the LLM's training cutoff. Without RAG, the LLM either admits ignorance or hallucinates plausible-sounding but incorrect answers about private knowledge.

whenWhy.title: Reducing hallucination on factual queries
whenWhy.body: LLMs hallucinate specific facts (numbers, names, dates, technical specifications). RAG reduces hallucination on the retrieved facts by grounding the answer in retrieved text. The LLM is prompted to answer 'based only on the provided context'. If the relevant document is retrieved, the LLM can cite it accurately; if it isn't retrieved (retrieval failure), the system should return 'I don't have information on this' rather than generating from model memory. This fail-safe is a critical design choice that must be implemented explicitly.

whenWhy.title: Scaling to large, dynamic document collections
whenWhy.body: Fine-tuning an LLM on a large document corpus: expensive (GPU time), requires retraining when documents change, may not inject specific facts reliably. RAG scales to millions of documents with constant inference-time cost (retrieval + one LLM call). Updating the knowledge base: add/update/delete documents in the vector store without any model training. The knowledge base is decoupled from the model, enabling independent updates to both.

deepDiveIntro: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an architectural pattern that grounds LLM responses in external, up-to-date knowledge rather than relying solely on parametric memory encoded during pre-training. At its core, RAG retrieves a set of relevant text passages from an external corpus at query time and conditions the LLM's generation on those passages — converting the LLM from a closed-book oracle into an open-book system with verifiable, citable answers. This approach sidesteps two fundamental limitations of pure LLMs: knowledge cutoffs (the model has no information about events after training) and hallucination on specific facts that were not reliably learned or have been forgotten during compression into model weights.

A production RAG system has two architectural phases. The offline ingestion phase transforms raw documents into a searchable vector index: documents are loaded, preprocessed, chunked into ~512-token passages, embedded with a dense encoder, and written to a vector store with rich metadata. This phase runs once at setup and incrementally as documents change. The online query phase operates at inference time: the user's query is embedded with the same encoder, the top-k most similar chunks are retrieved via approximate nearest-neighbour search, optionally re-ranked with a cross-encoder, and finally injected into a prompt that instructs the LLM to answer strictly from the provided context. Most production failures originate in the ingestion phase — poor chunking and mismatched embedding models — not in the generation step. [1]

deepDive.heading: Ingestion Pipeline: Critical Design Decisions
deepDive.body: Document loading: use source-specific loaders (pdfplumber for PDFs, BeautifulSoup for HTML) — generic loaders lose formatting. Preprocessing: clean headers/footers, extract tables separately (tables need structured representation, not raw text chunks). Chunking: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter (split on paragraph → sentence → word boundaries, in that priority) with chunk_size=512 tokens and chunk_overlap=50 tokens is the recommended default. Embedding: text-embedding-3-small (OpenAI, 1536d) or BGE-base-en-v1.5 (open-source, MTEB top-performer, 768d). Metadata: attach source URL, document title, section, page number, and creation date to every chunk — enables filtered retrieval. Vector store: pgvector (Postgres extension, no new infrastructure) for small-medium scale; Pinecone or Weaviate for large scale. [1]

deepDive.heading: Query Pipeline: Retrieval and Augmentation
deepDive.body: Query pre-processing: (1) Query expansion — generate 3-5 paraphrases of the query and retrieve for each; merge results with RRF; (2) HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings, Gao et al. 2022) — generate a hypothetical answer to the query, embed it, and use it for retrieval (more semantically aligned with relevant chunks than the raw question). Retrieval: k=20 initial candidates. Re-ranking: cross-encoder (ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 or Cohere Rerank API) re-scores the 20 candidates, return top-5. This two-stage retrieval (fast bi-encoder for recall, slow cross-encoder for precision) is the standard production pattern. Prompt construction: 'Answer based only on the following context. If the answer is not in the context, say so. Context: {chunks}. Question: {query}'. [2]

deepDive.heading: Failure Modes and Mitigations
deepDive.body: Most RAG failures are retrieval failures, not generation failures. Common failure modes: (1) Semantic mismatch — the query uses different vocabulary than the documents; fix: add BM25 sparse retrieval alongside dense retrieval (hybrid search) to handle exact keyword matches; (2) Chunk boundary problem — the answer spans a chunk boundary; fix: increase chunk overlap (100 tokens) or use parent-child chunking; (3) Multi-hop queries — the answer requires chaining across multiple documents; fix: multi-hop retrieval (use the first result to generate a follow-up query); (4) Context window overflow — too many retrieved chunks exceed the LLM's context budget; fix: re-rank and take only top-3 after re-ranking; (5) LLM ignores context — the LLM uses its training knowledge instead of the retrieved context; fix: prompt engineering ('Answer ONLY based on the provided context. Do NOT use prior knowledge.'). [1]

deepDive.heading: Hybrid Search: Dense + Sparse Retrieval Fusion
deepDive.body: Dense vector search excels at semantic matching but can miss exact-match queries where the user employs the same terminology as the document (product codes, person names, technical acronyms). BM25 sparse retrieval, by contrast, handles exact lexical overlap reliably but fails on paraphrase queries. Hybrid search combines both: run dense ANN search and BM25 in parallel, then merge the two ranked lists using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF_score = Σ 1/(k + rankᵢ), k=60). RRF requires no score normalisation — cosine similarity and BM25 TF-IDF scores are on incompatible scales, so rank-based fusion is more robust than linear interpolation. Empirically, hybrid search improves Hit@5 by 5-15% over dense-only retrieval on benchmarks with heterogeneous query types (BEIR). Production implementations: Weaviate and Elasticsearch both support hybrid BM25 + vector search natively; in pgvector + PostgreSQL, run both queries and merge in application code. Choosing the right fusion ratio is often dataset-specific — evaluate on a held-out query set. [1]

takeaways: RAG pipeline: ingestion (chunk → embed → store) + query (retrieve → re-rank → generate). [1]
Chunking defaults: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, chunk_size=512 tokens, chunk_overlap=50 tokens. [2]
Two-stage retrieval: k=20 bi-encoder (recall) → cross-encoder re-rank → top-5 (precision). [2]
Hybrid search (dense + BM25) handles vocabulary mismatch; RRF merges rankings. [1]
Most production failures are retrieval failures — invest in chunking quality and hybrid search first. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you handle multi-modal documents (PDFs with tables and images)?
edgeCases.a: Tables: extract tables separately from text using libraries like pdfplumber (Python) or Camelot. Convert tables to Markdown format before embedding — Markdown tables preserve structure in text. Store table chunks with metadata {type: 'table', source: doc, page: N}. Alternatively, convert tables to natural language summaries ('Table 3: Q3 revenue by region — North America: $2.3B, Europe: $1.1B'). Images: for text-bearing images (screenshots, diagrams with labels), use OCR (Tesseract, AWS Textract) to extract text. For semantic images (charts, photos): embed a caption generated by a multimodal LLM (GPT-4V, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) and store alongside the image. At retrieval time, the caption is matched semantically; at generation time, the image is included in the multimodal prompt. PDF-specific: always use pdfplumber over PyPDF2 — it preserves column layout and table structure.

edgeCases.q: How do you decide on chunk size and overlap for a specific document type?
edgeCases.a: Chunk size is a trade-off: small chunks (128 tokens) → precise retrieval (the relevant sentence is isolated), but the LLM gets less context for synthesis. Large chunks (1024 tokens) → more context, but retrieval is less precise (the relevant sentence is buried in 900 irrelevant tokens). Guidance by document type: (1) Technical documentation: 256-512 tokens — structured, dense information; small chunks isolate specific facts; (2) Legal/financial documents: 512-1024 tokens — context is critical for interpretation; sections shouldn't be separated; (3) Conversational text (chat logs, interviews): sentence-level chunking with a window of 5 sentences — conversations need adjacency; (4) Code: function-level chunking — never split a function across chunks. Overlap: 10-20% of chunk size. Always evaluate chunking choices empirically with a retrieval evaluation set before committing.

sources.label: Gao et al. (2023) — Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey
Gao et al. (2022) — Precise Zero-Shot Dense Retrieval without Relevance Labels (HyDE)
